===Parasites===
The [[feather mite]] ''[[Aralichus ambiguae]]'' (syn. [[Aralichus canestrinii|''A. canestrinii'']] ''[[pro parte]]'') was recovered from old museum specimens of ''Ara ambiguus'' collected in Costa Rica, Panama and Nicaragua. This is a tiny [[ectoparasite]] or possibly [[Commensalism|commensal]], likely, based on related species, inhabiting the wing feathers on the ventral surfaces of the [[Flight feather#Secondaries|secondaries]] and inner [[Flight feather#Primaries|primaries]] in the channels formed by adjacent [[Barb (feather)|barbs]]. It feeds on tiny fragments broken off from the feathers. It appears most closely related to ''[[Aralichus mexicanus]]'' of Mexican populations of the [[military macaw]] and ''A. canestrinii'' (''[[sensu stricto]]'') from the [[scarlet macaw]], differing noticeably in the much larger size of the females in this species.<ref name=Parasite/>

===Disease===
This species is known to suffer from [[proventricular dilatation disease]], also known as "macaw wasting disease", a fatal inflammatory disease of the nerves of the upper and middle digestive tract. It is typified by a swollen [[proventriculus]] and tiny lesions which appear in the [[ganglia]] and nerves, and the affected birds show abnormal movements and have problems feeding. The [[aetiology]] is unknown, but a virus is suspected. It is possibly a virus dubbed "[[avian bornavirus]]" of the [[Bornaviridae]] family, which has been recovered from tissue of victims.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Amy L Kistler|author2=Ady Gancz|author3=Susan Clubb|author4=Peter Skewes-Cox|author5=Kael Fischer|author6=Katherine Sorber|author7=Charles Y Chiu|author8=Avishai Lublin|author9=Sara Mechani|author10=Yigal Farnoushi|author11-link=Alexander L. Greninger|author11=Alexander Greninger|author12=Christopher C Wen|author13=Scott B Karlene|author14=Don Ganem|author15=Joseph L DeRisi |date=31 July 2008 |title=Recovery of divergent avian bornaviruses from cases of proventricular dilatation disease: Identification of a candidate etiologic agent |journal=Virology Journal |volume=5 |issue=88 |page=88 |doi= 10.1186/1743-422X-5-88|pmid=18671869 |pmc=2546392 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Cultural associations ==
With a 2004 resolution, the city council voted to consider the subspecies ''Ara ambiguus'' ssp. ''guayaquilensis'', known in the city as ''papagayo de Guayaquil'', as an emblematic symbol of [[Guayaquil]] in Ecuador.<ref name="Efemérides"/><ref name=Cornejo2015/> A July 2005 city ordinance declared it so.<ref name=Darwinnet/> A 12m high ceramic monument to this subspecies by the artist Juan Marcelo Sánchez was unveiled in the city in 2006.<ref name=Monga>{{cite web |url=https://es.mongabay.com/2017/05/ecuador-guayaquil-papagayo-extincion/ |title=Papagayo de Guayaquil: ave emblema de la ciudad bajo amenaza por la destrucción de su hábitat |last=Aguilar |first=Daniela |date=21 May 2017 |website=Mongabay |publisher=Mongabayorg Corporation |language=es |access-date=20 August 2019}}</ref>

The macaw was also declared an official symbol of the village of [[El Castillo (village)|El Castillo]], Nicaragua, in the 2000s.<ref name="FundaRío">{{cite web |url=http://www.fundaciondelrio.org/campanas/1 |title=Fundación Del Río |author=<!--Not stated--> |publisher=Fundación Del Río |language=es |access-date=23 August 2019}}</ref>

A festival organised by the [[Centro Científico Tropical]], the [[Fundación del Río]] and more recently [[The Ara Project]] promoting great green macaw conservation and bi-national relations is held each year since 2002 in alternatively Costa Rica and then Nicaragua. During the festival nest caretakers receive prizes for helping in the conservation of the species.<ref name="FundaRío"/><ref>{{cite web |url=https://vianica.com/headline/470 |title=Eighth Binational Festival of Macaws in Nicaragua |last=Membreño |first=Cinthia |date=21 May 2010 |website=ViaNica |publisher=Viamerica S.A. |access-date=21 August 2019}}</ref><ref name=CCTrelaciones>{{cite web |url=http://www.cct.or.cr/contenido/cct-retoma-relaciones-binacionales-para-el-programa-lapa-verde/ |title=CCT retoma relaciones binacionales para el Programa Lapa Verde |author=<!--Not stated--> |work=Centro Científico Tropical |date=April 2017 |language=es |access-date=21 August 2019}}</ref><ref name=CCTmacaw>{{cite web |url=http://www.cct.or.cr/contenido/our-programs/investigation/green-macaw/ |title=Green Macaw |date=2017 |publisher=Centro Científico Tropical |access-date=21 August 2019}}</ref> The 2018 event was planned for El Castillo, Nicaragua, while the 2017 festival was held in [[Río Cuarto District|Rio Cuarto]], Costa Rica.{{citation needed|date=August 2019}}

===Vernacular names===
In [[Spanish language|Spanish]] it is known as '''''guacamayo verdelimón'''''<ref name=LibRojoAvesColom/><ref name="Botero–Del2011"/> or '''''guacamayo verde mayor'''''<ref name="Efemérides"/><ref name=Darwinnet/> and locally as '''''lapa verde''''' in Costa Rica<ref name=Chassot2007/><ref name=Villegas-Retana2017/><ref name=CCTrelaciones/><ref name=LapasSkies>{{cite news |date=27 April 2017 |title=Lapas Begin To Fill The Skies of Costa Rica |work=The Costa Rica News |url=https://thecostaricanews.com/lapas-begin-fill-skies-costa-rica/}}</ref><ref name=Nicoya>{{cite news |date=28 January 2013 |title=Macaw Population Being Revitalized in Nicoya Peninsula |work=[[The Costa Rica Star]] |url=https://news.co.cr/macaw-population-being-revitalized-in-nicoya-peninsula/21701/}}</ref> and Nicaragua.<ref name="FundaRío"/><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.riosanjuan.com.ni/ruta-de-las-aves/lapa-verde/ |title=Lapa verde – Río San Juan |author=<!--Not stated--> |website=Río San Juan |publisher=Instituto Nicaragüense de Turismo |language=es |access-date=23 August 2019}}</ref>

The southern Ecuadorian population of ''Ara ambiguus'' ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' is locally known as '''''papagayo de Guayaquil''''' in Spanish.<ref name="Efemérides"/><ref name=Cornejo2015/><ref name=Darwinnet/>

===Aviculture===
This species is bred in captivity. A large enclosure of 15m in length is recommended for housing outside of the breeding season. The [[aviary]] should have a large tree trunk in the middle. It should not be kept indoors all the time. Healthy birds enjoy large chewable toys and weekly decorations of fresh branches of pine or eucalyptus in their enclosure. An overhead mister is needed for bathing. A recommended nest box is a 21in x 36in barrel.<ref name=ParrotEncyclo/> Different sources recommend different feeding regimes for captive birds. Important is soaked and/or sprouted seeds, as well as some fresh vegetables and fruit, along with nutritionally complete standard commercial macaw pellets.<ref name=ParrotEncyclo/><ref name=krauw/> Larger seeds, peanuts, acorns and other larger nuts are recommended, as well as a daily palm nut. It is best to sometimes supply some [[Gizzard|small bits of gravel]] to aid in digestion, and some extra calcium at regular times (especially for females). It is prone to biting people if not properly adjusted to humans from a young age.<ref name=krauw/>

==Conservation==
=== Status ===
This species of parrot is considered critically endangered by the [[IUCN]].<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" /> In 2001 Chassot ''et al.''. thought it should be considered at risk of extinction in [[Costa Rica]].<ref name=Monge2003/> The species is protected from international trade under [[CITES]] Appendix I. While conservation projects have had success educating people and releasing great green macaws into the wild, their numbers remained between 500 and 1000 individuals worldwide as of 2020.<ref name=Manza>{{Cite web | url=http://www.aramanzanillo.org |title = Ara Manzanillo – Macaw Reintroduction Project}}</ref> It is considered a [[vulnerable species]] in the 2002 [[Regional Red List|Red List]] of birds of [[Colombia]].<ref name="Botero–Del2011"/><ref name=SIRAP-Caribe/> The 2014 Colombian Red List upgrades it to [[Critically endangered species|critically endangered]], citing criteria A, the historical loss of habitat (46%, although the authors note the recuperation of 4.7% of the forests in the 2000–2010 period), and C, the potential reduction of the population in the future – it does not qualify for the other two criteria.<ref name=LibRojoAvesColom/>

The subspecies ''Ara ambiguus'' ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' is amongst the rarest parrots in the world. It is considered vulnerable by the IUCN (1996), and was included in the 2002 Red List of birds of [[Ecuador]] as [[critically endangered]].<ref name=Cornejo2015/>

=== Population statistics ===
Population estimates have been somewhat divergent. The first estimate of world totals of the wild population was 5,000 birds in 1993, 2,500 to 10,000 birds in 2000, and less than 2,499 in 2002 in the first [[Regional Red List|Red List]] of birds of Colombia.<ref name="Botero–Del2011"/> In 2004–2005 Jahn sent an unpublished estimate to [[Bird Life International]] (BLI) of 2,500 mature individuals, or some than 3,700 individuals including young, of which he believed 1,700 to 2,500 were to be found in the [[Panama]]-Colombia borderlands. BLI somehow derived an estimated total world population of 1,000 to 2,500 from that in 2005, and has maintained that number in subsequent assessments despite conflicting evidence.<ref name=":0" />

All these previous estimates were basically guesses, but in 2009 Monge ''et al.''. performed calculations using known population densities, satellite imagery and the known ranges, and estimated a total population of 7,000, of which 1,530 were to be found in Costa Rica and the southeastern portion of [[Nicaragua]],<ref name=Chassot2012 /><ref name=":0" /> and 302 in Costa Rica.

An unreferenced global population estimate by the [[American Bird Conservancy]] in 2016 put the population at 3,500.<ref name=abc>{{Cite web|url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/great-green-macaw/|title=American Bird Conservancy I Bringing Back the Birds|website=American Bird Conservancy|language=en-US|access-date=2017-12-19}}</ref>

In the second Red List of birds in Colombia in 2014, 3,385 birds were calculated for that country, using 1/4 lower population density statistics than normal for a number of reasons, but even then the authors state their belief that this is an overestimation, and find a population of 2,500 mature birds in the country more likely. This number includes an estimate of 1,700 birds found in the Colombian part of the Darién region made in the same work.<ref name=LibRojoAvesColom/> In 1994, the population of macaws in Costa Rica was estimated by Monge ''et al.''. to be at 210 individuals with only 35 to 40 breeding pairs.<ref name=Chassot2012 /> The estimated population in Costa Rica and southern Nicaragua was calculated to be 1530 individuals by Monge ''et al.'' in 2009.<ref name=Chassot2012 /><ref name=":0" /> An adjusted estimate of 350 in Costa Rica in 2019 has been derived from that total by including released birds bred in captivity.<ref name=Manza/>

The population trend would appear to show an increasing population, but due to the undependable nature of the earlier assessments such a conclusion would appear premature.

In the 2002 Red List of birds of Ecuador, the total population was estimated at between 60 and 90 individuals,<ref name=Monga/> and an unpublished estimate by Horstman for BLI in 2012 was of only 30 to 40 individuals.<ref name=":0" /> Only twelve wild macaws were thought to exist of the southern population of the [[endemic]] ''Ara ambiguus'' ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' in 1995.<ref name=Darwinnet/> In the early 2010s a flock of 36 birds was seen in [[Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco|Río Canandé Reserve]] in northern Ecuador, disproving the low estimate.<ref name=abc/>

=== Threats ===
The main threat for the survival of the great green macaw was habitat loss. It is estimated that between 1900 and 2000 some 90% of the original habitat has been lost in [[Costa Rica]].<ref name=":2"/> Private land not owned by the government is or has been developed into agricultural fields for the production of crops such as [[Elaeis guineensis|oil palm]], [[pineapple]]s and [[banana]]s.<ref name=Chassot2012 /><ref name=":2" /> Especially in the 1980s and 1990s the unsustainable harvest of ''[[Dipteryx oleifera]]'' and other trees that produce high quality wood is thought to have further compromised macaw habitat, as only 30% of the remaining rainforest in the northeast is thought to be [[Old-growth forest|primary]].<ref name=":2" /> As of 2015 [[Indio Maíz Biological Reserve|Indio-Maíz Biological Reserve]] in Nicaragua is threatened by settlers moving into the reserve to found farms, especially of subsidence agriculture, oil palm and cattle.<ref name=Salvemos>{{cite web |url=https://en.salvemoslareservaindiomaiz.org/ |title=Salvemos la Reserva Indio Maíz, pulmón de Centroamérica |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2019 |website=Salvemos la Reserva Indio Maíz |access-date=21 August 2019}}</ref> Costa Rican loggers continued to cross the border to illegally harvest timber in the reserve as of 2007.<ref name=Chassot2007/>

Other threats have included hunting pressure for sport and the feathers, and the pet trade, with chicks fetching prices of up to $300 in Costa Rica in 2001.<ref name=":2" />

[[Hurricane Otto]] of November 2016, which crossed Central America into the Pacific directly through the Nicaragua-Costa Rica border region, has had a large effect on the woodlands and communities of the region. Three nests were destroyed.<ref name=CCTrelaciones/> Dead wood left in the forests after the hurricane fuelled large forest fires in Indio-Maíz, Nicaragua, in 2018,<ref>{{cite web |url=https://vianica.com/headline/1120 |title=Fire in Indio – Maiz Biological Reserve |last=Solórzano Canales  |first=Róger |date=5 April 2018 |website=ViaNica |publisher=Viamerica S.A. |access-date=21 August 2019}}</ref> destroying 5,500 hectares.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2018/04/10-killed-unrest-intensifies-nicaragua-180422060137453.html|title=At least 10 killed as unrest intensifies in Nicaragua|last=Gies|first=Heather|date=22 April 2018|publisher=Al Jazeera|access-date=2018-04-23}}</ref>

In southern Ecuador it was reported in 2000 that capture of chicks of ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' for national commerce continued to be a problem, at times by attempting to fell trees to get at the nest. An indication of this is the reported ownership of at least 20 pet birds of this species in Guayaquil alone in 1997. Local residents of the area around [[Cerro Blanco Forest]] report the macaws are [[Pest (organism)|pests]] on maize cultivation. They are known to have been killed as an agricultural pest in [[Esmeraldas Province]], at least in the 1990s.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=Darwinnet/> They have also been killed for food.<ref name=Darwinnet/>

=== Protection ===
====Honduras====
It occurs in the [[Río Plátano Biosphere Reserve]], where it is rare, in eastern [[Honduras]].<ref name=":0" /> It has also been seen in the hills of the [[Sierras del Warunta]] within the proposed [[Rus-rus Biological Reserve]].<ref name=Chassot2011/>

====Costa Rica and Nicaragua====
In Costa Rica commerce of the macaw was reduced after an environmental education program was initiated in 1998 by George Powell and his research team.<ref name=CCTmacaw/> In 1998 this research team, later united as [[Centro Científico Tropical]], devised a conservation plan with an alliance of 18 different organizations known as the San Juan-La Selva Biological Corridor which would protect the habitat of the great green macaw.<ref name=Chassot2012 /><ref name=CCTmacaw/> An earlier iteration of this plan had first been proposed in 1985 by the first revolutionary [[Nicaraguan Revolution|Sandinista government]] in the midst of the US-sponsored [[Contras]] insurgency, as an "international ecological peace park" (SI-A-PAZ), but the binational agreements with the Costa Rican government were never carried out, so instead Nicaragua established the vast "Áreas Naturales Protegidas del Sureste de Nicaragua" in the southeast, and a similar block of land in the northeast bordering Honduras. After the elections the new Nicaraguan government reduced and carved up these blocks of land between 1997 and 1999, which then became a number of new and much smaller reserves. Much of this land was actually set aside in 1987 to be governed by the indigenous population of these regions, such as the [[Rama people|Rama]] and [[Belizean Kriol people|Kriol]] people, which has created legal conflict.<ref name=Chassot2007/><ref name=Salvemos/><ref name="BR">{{cite book |author=McGinnis, Michael D. |title=Bioregionalism |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1999 |pages=176–177 |isbn=0-415-15444-8 }}</ref> The [[Indio Maíz Biological Reserve|Indio-Maíz Biological Reserve]] remains the main refuge for this species in the two countries.<ref name=Chassot2007/> The new "biological corridor plan" entailed the creation of the [[Maquenque National Wildlife Refuge]] in Costa Rica in 2005, which helps connect the six previously existing protected areas of the [[Tortuguero National Park]] and [[La Selva Biological Station]] in the [[Cordillera Central (Costa Rica)|Cordillera Central]] in Costa Rica, with the [[Barra del Colorado Wildlife Refuge]], the [[Indio Maíz Biological Reserve|Indio-Maíz Biological Reserve]], [[Punta Gorda Natural Reserve]] and the [[Cerro Silva Natural Reserve]] in Nicaragua, thereby allowing animals to move between the regions. The plan was considered a success in 2012.<ref name=Chassot2012 /><ref name=CCTmacaw/> The macaws migrate to the mountains in northern central Costa Rica after breeding, for example to [[Braulio Carrillo National Park]].<ref name=Chassot2007/> A national prohibition of the cutting of ''almendro de montaña'' (''Dipteryx oleifera'') trees was also engineered by the Centro Científico Tropical.<ref name=CCTmacaw/> Experimental ''D. oleifera'' plantations have also been established around [[Sarapiquí (canton)|Sarapiquí]], which appear to show the species is acceptable for commercial [[silviculture]].<ref name=Gómez2009/> The Costa Rican [[NGO]] [[The Ara Project|Ara Manzanillo]] has released 60 [[captive breeding|captive-bred]] birds in [[Jairo Mora Sandoval Gandoca-Manzanillo Mixed Wildlife Refuge]] near [[Puerto Viejo de Talamanca]] (Old Harbour), southeasternmost coastal Costa Rica, as of 2019.<ref name=Manza/>

In Nicaragua there are further populations in the east of the country in the [[Bosawás Biosphere Reserve|Bosawás]] and [[Río San Juan Wildlife Refuge|San Juan reserves]].<ref name=":0" /> [[Fundación del Río]] is an organisation which carries out macaw conservation in southeast Nicaragua.<ref name="FundaRío"/>

[[Image:Barra del Colorado Waterway.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Riverine habitat in [[Barra del Colorado Wildlife Refuge]].]]

====Panama====
It is reasonably common in parts of [[Darién National Park]].<ref name=":0" />

====Colombia====
It is common in [[Utría National Natural Park]] along the Pacific coast (as of 2003).<ref name=":0" /> It also occurs and is protected in [[Los Katíos National Park]] bordering Darién in Panama, [[Paramillo National Park]], [[Sanquianga National Park]] and in southwest of the country in [[Farallones de Cali#National Park and Biodiversity|Farallones de Cali National Park]].<ref name="Botero–Del2011"/>

====Ecuador====
The southern Ecuadorian population of ''Ara ambiguus'' ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' is mostly protected in the [[Cerro Blanco Forest]] just west of the city of [[Guayaquil]], a private reserve administered by the Ecuadorian NGO [[Fundación Pro-Bosque]], which is expanding the plantings of native trees on the grounds. The [[Jambeli Foundation]] undertakes captive reproduction near the city, and a number of municipal organisations such as Parque Historico, the Urban Parks and Public Spaces Administration and the [[Guayaquil Botanical Garden]] undertake educational activities related to this bird.<ref name=Monga/><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.guayaquilesmidestino.com/en/nature/flora-and-fauna/parrot-of-guayaquil |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160403180853/http://www.guayaquilesmidestino.com/en/nature/flora-and-fauna/parrot-of-guayaquil |url-status=usurped |archive-date=3 April 2016 |title=Parrot of Guayaquil – Welcome to Guayaquil, Official tourism website |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2019 |website=Guayaquil es mi Destino |publisher=Empresa Pública Municipal de Turismo, Promoción Cívica y Relaciones Internacionales de Guayaquil, EP |access-date=20 August 2019}}</ref> It is used as a flagship species for conservation of the fragmented remnants of the dry forest ecosystem only found near this city.<ref name=Cornejo2015/>

Between 2017 and 2019 fourteen birds of ssp. ''guayaquilensis'' captive-bred by the Jambeli Foundation and [[Loro Parque|Loro Parque Fundación]] were released into the private [[Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco|Ayampe Reserve]] in [[Esmeraldas Province]] owned by the [[Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco]].<ref name=WLT/> It is possible that by doing so they are mixing up populations of the subspecies, as it is unclear if the original population in Esmeraldas is not the nominate.

The northern Ecuadorian population is primarily protected within the [[Cotacachi Cayapas Ecological Reserve]] where most of the population is thought to be found,<ref name=Darwinnet/> it is also found within the [[Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco|Río Canandé Reserve]], another private reserve owned by the Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco.<ref name=abc/>

zh.extract:
大绿金刚鹦鹉（学名：Ara ambiguus；英語：Great Green Macaw，Buffon's Macaw）生活在哥伦比亚、哥斯达黎加、巴拿马以及尼加拉瓜等地。成年体长85厘米。身体主要为黄绿色，与军舰金刚鹦鹉很接近，但本种颜色略浅。前额有小片红色羽毛，脸颊两侧有黑色条纹状羽毛，飞羽及尾内侧羽为橄榄黄，黑色喙，浅黄色虹膜。野外状态下，主要活动在森林低地、季节性干燥的地区，喜群居。食物包括水果、坚果、浆果、种子及植物嫩芽。
繁殖期在每年的1至4月，每次产卵1-3枚，孵化期26天，羽化期12周。
